speaker: interviewer
text: --Abraham Melezin. Dr. Melezin, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: We're on our way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dr. Melezin, you spoke about coming to the United States. Did you get married when you came to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got married on February 29, 1948. It's a joke with us. Look, I've known her for a long time. I won't have money to buy presents for every anniversary. So-- but once in four years, maybe I'll save.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you and, and your wife go to live in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the problem was, you know, in my family in Poland, there was my sister and my brother. I was the giver. Essentially, I put them through the school and through the-- whatever they did, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here, my brother-in-law was a very rich man, was very impressed that there is, in the family, two professors. So he wanted us-- to keep us for two years. I should study English and then go on, of course, to teach at university. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I take money after so many years I was giving money? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Hence, the very same attitude toward Rachela. You know, she never-- she was very independent, very good. So what to do? I told Rachela at that time that I can go on the corner and sell ties, you know, in order to-- What did you do together?

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I went, you know, she-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] she met, in Sweden, a Jewish farmer from Galicia who bought here a chicken farm. And because I was essentially brought up on a farm-- so we decided that this is one way out. But there was more serious problems, you know. Here was Rachela, a grown-up adult with definite ideas and definite point of view, you know, worldview. And here was Abraham, who had the very same, you know-- went through everything, definite ideas, all right. And although we knew each other, essentially we were intellectually strangers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then there was Sarah-- Sarah, who choose me to be her father, who, who has chosen me, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was Rachela's daughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachela told this story?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, perhaps you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- and I am a-- definitely a family man. Whatever happened in my family between my mother and my father gave me a lesson once and forever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got married. We decided to build a family. We have to be together on the farm. We went on the farm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was another moment, you know. When I came back from concentration camp, I felt that I lost-- this is what you call aesthetic posture. I was brutalized. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought that on the farm, I'll regain something from the nature. And so happened that we bought a farm which was not a farm, but it was an estate with beautiful surroundings, which absolutely cured, you know, us. At least, I know that I regained my-- this is what I call aesthetic posture. How long were you on the farm, Dr. Melezin?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the farm-- the farm-- on this farm, I start in '48 and-- with partnership with that man from Galicia. Then we parted. In 1950, we got so-called our farm. We built up a farm with capacity about 20,000, 25,000 chickens, with a cow, with machinery, with 200 acres of land. I plowed. I did-- planted. I harvested.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after that, you went into teaching?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you teach?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, you see, I came with an idea-- well, farm-- so I would be a kind of poor, so to speak, you know. So I'll be independent. I'll be able to go and teach wherever I want-- whenever I want and wherever I want. So turned out that every chicken on the farm and every worker on the farm was-- were my bosses. And I couldn't move. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then there was a variety of things. Rachela started-- what's-- it was her initiative. She said, look, you are not going to be a farmer. We are not going to build farms everywhere. It is dead-end street. Go and teach. I'll become a farmer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got first job in the evening division of City College. When the geographer took me to the chairman of the evening division and told him who I am, he said, grab him, you know. I have, in Poland, already 15 published works, you know. So you're overqualified a little bit. I didn't like too much teaching in the City College because it was department of economics. And there was opening in department of Trenton. I moved there. It was '58, '59. I moved '59, '60 to Trenton. They start me on the lowest possible level. And within one-half-- one semester, they advanced me to associate professorship with full scholarship. And then I published a piece about the Soviet Union. And everybody went after me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I must tell you one thing. Rachela took over the farming. She was the farmer. And the very first day when I went teaching-- I went-- I went to New York teaching-- she succeeded to, to put her hand into a machine and almost lost her hand. Well, it is a long story to tell it. Anyway, well, let me tell--

speaker: interviewer
text: After Trenton State, did you teach anywhere else-- after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Trenton State, I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the City College bought me out, so to speak. I got almost a double salary, but rank-- lower rank. I didn't care too much, you know. I said, I'll put in the foot, and [? everything that. ?] And this what has happened. I, I didn't know to remain in Trenton State with lower salary or to go to City College. But my son-in-law came. He said, I will give my right hand to teach in the City College to those geniuses. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, he's American boy. He graduated Columbia. But he would give a right hand to teach in the City College? So I can give up rank and go with both hands there. [LAUGHS] Have you retired from your teaching at City College now?

speaker: interviewee
text: They retired me in 1980 because I hit 70. And, you know, in the United States we can be president when we are 76 and sleep during the meetings-- sleep during the meetings. [LAUGHS] But you can't teach. Although, in two years later I could stay. But the department and students tried to influence the dean-- no. And I'm very happy that I retired. Because I retired just in time, because between 1970 and 1980, the City College turned into the second great high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at the end of your testimony, just about, Dr. Melezin, and I just wondered if there was any message that you would like to give on this tape to your daughter, to your granddaughter, to the people who are watching the tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] Look, [CRYING] to Sarah and Alex, I want to tell them only one thing. Remain Jewish. And be proud that you are Jewish. [CRYING] Otherwise, you know, when I look back on all those things-- so I see a series of independent events, sometimes interdigitated, sometimes accidental. No matter what I would do, there was very little input on my side, you know, for survival. I jumped from the train. Probably this is the biggest input. Otherwise, everything did-- other people did. Krzanowski, Felix Lada in concentration camp. In the City College-- in the City-- Professor [INAUDIBLE] in Trenton State College. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the City College, some colleagues of mine. Felix Lada in-- I think I told you the name already-- in Stutthof, you know. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I know that you-- I agree with Rachela that this was an earthquake. And, you know, and we shouldn't dwell on it. It is what happened to us. And I, I tried. But, you know, Sarah, when I saw her the first time in Lodz, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she's only three months older-- was theoretically three months older than my son. For me, this is like materialization of my child.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Would you like to introduce us to Sarah's daughter, Alex? We can ask her to come into the room.

speaker: interviewee
text: One thing, Alex, darling, what I want to tell you. You know, I faced death, real death, for three or four times. And I never gave up. And in your life, in your career, never give up. There is always a way out. And there is always the light at the end of the tunnel, Alex. Because there is no tunnel without a light, without an end.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Alex, how do you feel about what your grandfather is saying to you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: She's crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] How do I feel about what he's saying? He-- he's said these words to me before. And this is really one of the first times that I've heard his story. I've heard bits and pieces, but he's not been as vocal as my grandmother has. So I just find him-- both of my grandparents-- very inspiring people. And they've been a source of inspiration throughout my life. [CRYING] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was nine years old when I found out that this man, who I call Papa, wasn't my real grandfather-- whatever that means, 'real.' He's not my biological grandfather. And when my mom told me, I was-- I was shocked. I mean, I, I, I guess I can only kind of compare it to when she found out that her mother wasn't really who, who she thought she was. But I can't imagine having a better grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Alex. And thank you Dr. Melezin for giving us your testimony today.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I'm sorry that I got emotional. In, in my 85 years, or let's say the last 50 years that I looked all the way back to my childhood and everything, you know, up to the last day, you know, till today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your testimony was very important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And let me tell you one thing. If I am-- [CRYING] if I am alive, whatever I am now-- not only in professional terms, but as a human being-- I owe to a high degree to Rachela. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is a picture of me when I was 19 years old, graduating from the high school before I entered--

speaker: interviewee
text: You sure?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I am sure. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of, of me, September 1936. This is my wife, Sulamite, and I, and it is exactly about one week after our wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Zarek, born 7th of January, 1941-- no, this is 1940. He is probably here about five or six months old. I don't know why his nanny sent me only one picture, whereas I had of them-- a couple dozen of them, probably, already at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and we're on our way again.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a spoon and a fork that I acquired for probably three or four pieces of bread in Stutthof. And here, on the handle there is my number, 32,209. I made it when I worked in the armory.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you just get it all in a block, that would be fine, too, because I don't think-- you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: You're not going to be able to read it anyway.

words.text: to be able to read it anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: --read it, anyway. Right, exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And any time. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, this is a part of a notebook that I read in a concentration camp at the peril of my own health, you know. In order to divert attention from all the terrible things around us, we tried to do something. I tried to divert attention of my colleagues on the block.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- and they knew already who I am-- who I was. And they-- I started to teach them geography, geology, meteorology. Right now, here is a page which describes the change in the temperature with changes of altitude.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to tell you that the book note-- they provided me. It is the original one. And it was hidden because if they would-- if the Gestapo would find, I would be immediately shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's in English probably already, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is that the English lesson?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you're learning English here, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're ready?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I took lessons of English from other things, you know. This is a reciprocation from a Danish or Norwegian man. And here I wrote-- he dictated me English words. And I translated-- and I translated them into Polish because he, he had a small dictionary-- small dictionary-- English, Danish, or German. But I understand. And I would put it in-- for example, on, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I don't think we'll be able to quite read all that. So stand by-- and any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, this is the striped suit that we, we used in Stutthof, essentially. This is not the original one because the original one was much shorter, shorter sleeves. But later, when they gave, gave it on a purpose, you know-- not the same size. But a colleague of mine, a Dane in Schneiderei-- it means in the 'tailor' Kommando-- made it for me to measure. And in order to be warmer, he added in the lining in the-- in the sleeves-- in the sleeves. There was a number above the pocket. But when the Soviets came and they took it into de-lousing, into the steam boiler. So I don't remember how it was attached you know. But it disappeared. But the number was a white piece of cloth with a triangle facing down, with letter P-- it means the Pole, Pole-- and the number 32209.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any time.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, in Stutthof, like in probably in other concentration camps, there was Underground. So we had radio possibility. We knew what's going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the kapo of block five brought me this map. His name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And I kept it between the boards of the wall. And at nighttime, we would follow the events of the war in Africa, in, in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the D-Day, he woke me up at night. And I thought that I'm going to die. Because at night, they used to wake up only those who has to be executed. And then he said, take the map. They landed. All right. And then we followed, of course, you know, the events of the Soviets when we were evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a radio then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: A radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a radio at least in three places. Probably block seven, in Arbeiteinsatz, and on block five has one. This is a very peculiar group of Poles, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. When we were evacuated on January 25, in the morning, we went out from the concentration camp. So we heard the bombardment-- artillery-- coming, coming from northeast. And we heard the bombardment for about a full day. At night, we already were too far. Or they stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: It is very important for me--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, I need to restart. Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a very interesting and important document. When I went on the Aryan side, I assumed the name of Adam Mielzynski. The family Mielzynski is aristocratic family from Posen district. They had stables in a place called [NON-ENGLISH]. When I came to Stutthof, so-- the people from Pomerania and Voidevoship of Posen assumed that I am one of the Mielzynskis. Essentially, they thought that I am the [NON-ENGLISH] Mielzynski. And this document states that Adam Mielzynski, who permanently lives in this particular place, goes with his own horse to Bialystok Voivodeship to find the family, and they ask all the authorities to help me. And this could be only made for a Count Adam Mielzynski. It is the first picture of our trio here in the United States, at the roof of 771 West 10th Avenue-- Rachela, I, and Sarah. And I must tell you one thing, that Sarah was very jealous. She couldn't understand my relationship to Rachela. And she wanted to have the same relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, she told Rachela that it is not she or she alone, but I am going to best her. And this what was-- for a year or so, we would have a night shower or night bath together. And she permitted-- got permission to call me Abrasha. And she felt that she possesses me. She did. She does.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHTER] All right, the idea of going on the farm was to create-- to build a family. And here is one example. Whatever I did or Rachela did on the farm, Sarah-- if she was not in the school-- right was there. Here, I remember I had some injections-- inject the chickens. Sarah would collect eggs, would crawl under the coops to take the [? previous ?] eggs, the smallest ones. And she would grade the eggs, and for her, it was as important as going to school. And typical working day-- it's summertime-- and we really built a family. OK, this is my mother in 1986, when she was in Los Angeles. At that time, she was probably about 95, maybe 96 years old. My mother died in January 1989. And she was about 98, maybe 100 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once I asked, mother, how old are you? She said, son, at this age, what's the difference a year here, a year there? She lived to a very old age. And she was, you know, one of few Jewish mothers who had-- all her three children survived the Holocaust.
